r, | J. C. LYONS AND THE LEDESTON PRESS. e
The memory of John Charles Lyons and the Ledeston Press, one i
q, of the very few private presses in Ireland has almost died out of ot
I"a existence. Yet he was a man of really unique character and ability, : ?
:sd well-deserving of remembrance. The son of Capt. C. J. Lyons, by Mary ,1
' Anne, daughter of Sir Richard Levinge, he was born on 22nd August, ;j
1792, at Ledeston on the western shore of historic' Ennel, near Mullin- LT
0. gar, and, as he himself states in his ‘“ Grand Juries of Westmeath”’ = t"ﬁ
L descended from an old protestant, or as they were denominated  Hu- ;::i
guenot race,” he entered Pembroke College, Oxford, 25th May, 1810. iR 'iﬁ
el, Leaving the University after a rather uneventful course,  he w’%
lfe | entered into possession: of the family estates which he in- “5
| herited seven years before, on the death of his grandfather. ::% i
He always had a passion for collecting records, especi- - o g
ally those relating to county families, and John O’Donovan in his Ord- = :*3
nance Survey letters on Westmeath, refers to him as an authority on |
local topography. -
He was a practical working gardener and also a skilled
86, l mechanician, self-taught; in fact he might be. called a born genius in
Bt | that respect. The press with which he printed some of his books, and
'i,: the machine with which he bound them, were his own handiwork.:
:di- g He made several clocks, all of them -of excellent workman-
: | ship, and good timekeepers. Indeed the only relics of thlS remark--
[ 8ble man at present in the family mansion at Ledeston are four or
i | five of his clocks, and the remains of his library. A clock presented
008 by him to. the Mullmgar Board of Guardxans. of whwh body he was- 7f
eeﬁ Chairman, for about:25 years, still remains in a prominent posmlon 2
:;’ | . in the board. room. It bears the. iolluwmg inscription: *‘ Made and. - ;_;__j;_ £
30 | Presented by J. C: Lyons, Chairman to the Board of Guardians of the --
ot | Mlﬂllggar Union, 1850.” He was.the last of the old Seneschals ofes Liisiaih

Mullmgar and religiously prasided every Saturday at the- Manor 5 3
Court, until its abolition in 1837, _Both in. the B_oard Room and m the-e = -« R
~anor Court he was always gmde “‘ iren

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER.

-7
Lo

There is an odd number of a Magazine in the same year called “ The
Inspector.”” Both these, of course, had some political significance and
object. In 1750, we come on ‘‘ The Mirror,” of which two numbers are
extant, and appeared in November oi that year. It must have lasted
tant, and appeared in November of that year. It must have lasted
some months as it provoked a rival journal called “ A Looking-glass
for the Mirror,” No. 1 of which appeared in July, 1751. There are only
two numbers of it in the collection. |

The following year and running into 1753, there was a magazine
called “ The Covent Garden Journal,”” perhaps a reprint of the London
publication oi the same mname. Then we have ¢ The Dublin
Spy,” that started in August, 1753, which used to appear
on Fridays, and the first number of which (August 13th)
was published by Thomas Hutchinson at the Reindeer in Charles
Street, opposite Mountrath Street, while the third number was printed
by James Byrne, of Thomas Street, for James Eyre Weeks, who was the
original author and proprietor. “The Dublin Spy >’ went on for several

‘months, the last date on the extant numbers being the 27th of May,

1754 and was No. LV. _
Next in 1753 we have “ The Dublin Joker,” which appeared in Sep-

tember of that year. In the following October appeared  The Censor.”
Tt had a longer career evidently than some of the others, as the extant
issue is No. 95 of Vol. II. In January, 1754, appeared “ The Trifler,”
and in the following June, “ The City Watchman.”” The last in the
collection is called ‘ Every Man’s Journal,” and it appeared in Oc-
tober, 1765, and was printed by James Byrne, of Cook Street. Iive
numbers are forthcoming. Most of these magazines are large 4to Or

~ small folio in size,jan’d' consist of 4 pp. only, and generally contain two

¥

columns in each page. .
There is one other item which is very curious in title and appear-

ance and which I kept for mention till' the last. It is entitled: “A
hfofsel from the Wolf in Bloudy Sheep’s Clothing.”” It appeared on
h epf 1753, and was printed by Thomas Hutchinson.
1ty the entire i8

stence of these Journals-
est and lead to other
or similar journals. It would require an abler
than mine to deal properly with these strang®

- This is 'b,_iit. a very bare mention of the exi

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER. 73

and rare magazines, to do justice to their contents or to fully set forth
their value and importance from a historical point of view. I hope
it may be done some time. It'is indeed lamentable®to’think- of the
number of our magazines of the kind which have been lost.
E. R. Mc. C. DIX.
GOSSIP.

There i1s a capital portrait of Mr. A. P. Graves in the December
number of “ The Book Monthly,” and a spirited poem, which we take
to be a translation from theCymrie, by him, entitled ‘‘Taliesyn’s
Song to the Wind,” in “ The Athenaeum >’ for 27th November,. In
“The ‘Academy >’ for 4th of December, Mr. H. Sirr falls foul of Mr.
Stephen Gwynn, M.P., for ‘“ what purports to be a statement of fact
which concerns the honour of Major Sirr, my grandfather,”” in his
recant historical romance ‘“ Robért Emmet,”” and quotes from Mad-
den’s * Life  (1846), that the Major was “ civil and coaxing !’

The articles of interest to Irish readers in the December Magazines
are Sir Robert Anderson’s, continuation in ‘ Blackwood>’> of his
“Lighter Side of Official Life,” in which he makes the bold asser-
tions that penal servitude changed Michael Davitt from an ignorant
lout,” and ‘“ that it was in a'convict prison Davitt acquired his fitnesa
for the part he afterwards played as Parnell’s ally. in the Irish Land
War, and he freely acknowledged this upon occasions?”’ In the same
number Col. St. Quintin tells in rattling style the'life story. of his
famous racehorse, Ballyraggett, “ Old Bally.” ' Col. Pilkington (Pat-
rick Perterras) discusses ‘‘ Irish Polidy and the Conservatives>’ in the

“ Nineteenth Century and After,”’ proving to his own satisfaction; at
least, that “ Codlin’s the friend; not Short.”” In the “ Quarterly Maga-

2ine,” Dr. Grattan Flood writes on - Dublin City Music,”’ between
1456 and 1786, his material bemg denved from the Calendar of Anclent,

Records. - ¢

published by subscription, price 10s. The volume will be demy 8o,
about 350 pp., with about 50 illustrations, some of them full page. The
specimen illustrations he encloses are very interesting and well Pro-
duced.

We are delighted to learn that the good folks of Belfast have de-
clded to celebrate the centenary of their fellow-citizen, Sir Samuel]
Ferguson in March next with becoming eclat. Ferguson, undoubtedly
the greatest Irish poet of the latter half of the nineteenth century was
born in Belfast, in 1810, a descendant of the “ Willy Gilliland,”’ whose
prowess he sang in one of his earliest ballads. He lies buried at Done-
gore in County Antrim—that hillside, the rallying-ground of the rem-
nant of the defeated rebel ranks in ’98, which he ‘‘ held not lightly,*
and preferred as his burial place, to the storied walls of Westminster
Abbey. Mr. Alired P. Graves is to pronounce the eulogium, and no more
fitting selection could be made.

Professor Leeper, of Trinity College, Melbourne, writes in “ N. &
Q.” that he has solved the mystery of the burial-place of SBwift’s “ Van-
Last year he saw in the Probate Court in Dublin, ‘“ an entry
_ showing that Vanessa was
buried there,”” and desires to place the fact on record.

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER. 75

gso much read by our people to-day, and well and worthily it fulfils its
task. Here is literary pabulum for all classes of readers—every word
of it interesting. The biographical sketches are well done, and much
of the verse i1s above the average—tender and touching. We gladly

welcome such an effort in a provincial town, and whilst wishing it a_

long and prosperous career, trust that other centres will follow the
excellent example of Clonmel. :
Mr. T. B. Mosher, of Portland, Maine, U.S.A,, sends us a beauti-

fully got up little volume in a neat case, as charming a booklet as it -

has been our lot to handle for a long time. It is entitled “ A Little Book

for John O’Mahoney’s Friends” (16mo. 10 pp., + 56, and is a reprint -

of the “ Memory ” contributed to the * Cornhill >>.in J anuary, 1906, by

Katherine Tynan, with some elegaic poems from the same svmpathe-

.jc-ic pen, in recollection of her brother-in-law, the giited young Cork
journalist and barrister, who passed away all too soon. It is cer-
tainly a graceful and loving tribute to the departed: The
same firm also issue -in similar style “A Little . Book

of XXIV. Carols” (small 4to., wili ~ + 40) By the
sam'e authoress, which will enhance her already lijgh reptuation, 950 2
coples only have been printed, on Van Gelder hand-made paper, and
a8 the type has been distributed, it is bound to become sought after
In the future, but meantime will make a most appropriate: Chl':iSt':j.' i‘:{

mas present,! -

ful, impartial record of a notable career; but when the t'a?{l_: is under--

taken, as in this case, by one so intimately associated with the subject,

, compelled to submit to his examination; and while the Irish Church 1
‘ lost, or rather, was the poorer through his conscientious inability to

: enter within her pale, it is not too much to say that organised reli-
gion has gained by his researches into the bases of morals, and his
cold, clear criticism of all forms of authority.

Lecky was educated at Cheltenham and Trinity College, Dublin
where he became a member of the Historical Society and a warm ad-
mirer of Dr. Ingram. His first work ‘The Reirgious Tendencies of
the Age,”” was published at the age of 22, and though somewhat crude
and sketchy, yet showed a spirit of lofty detachment which character-
ised him to the end. He left college without any distinct leaning to
a special career;and spent several years as a travelling student. Un. j

like the majority of the world’s great thinkers he was not dependent
for his daily bread upon his pen; and was able to wander up and
down Europe, delving in libraries, discovering unheard-of ‘tomes of
rare and curious learning which he put to such admirable use In his
great works on ‘‘ Rationalism ” and * European Morals.” g

" But wanderer as he was, he ever returned to his native isle, for no l
all-the beauty of Italy and Spain touched his nature so deeply 8s

Ireland.

The Memoir enters very fully into Lecky’s literary Iabours-—lf'--
bours astounding in unwearied assiduity in search of truth. And In
none of his works is his great industry more manifest than in th?st?
volmﬁes of his ““ History,”” which deal with Ireland. Facts Whldl
had been accepted for years were overthrown, views which had beeg

 enunciated by Froude and others were shown to be erroneous an

unjust. His impartiality and accuracy were never dispuiiedf' e

- His early works were sufficiently powerful to bring him into 5

centre of select literary circles. He became the intimate o.f FIerbB;?é
Spencer, Tennyson, Carlyle. The letters which contain remmlsceni: uf
of Carlyle are especially valuable as revéaling in that rugged'*-,scﬂ n-

~ature that could be even tender and charming. Lecky's 'hfe cod E
tinued along its even temour until the year 1881 when the Irish Larn

IrisE BrograraY.—Your readers may not be aware that there are hf
hundreds, if not thousands, of notices of Irishmen in Mr. Frederic | ‘i
Boase’s ““ Modern -English Biography.”” This great work, however, 11:;
only deals with those persons who have died since 1850. It is pub- 1
lished in 3 vols. (1892-1901), ior the author, by Netherton and Worth, i *

was introduced into Trim in the year 1835. The first printer was the- ’f
Henry Griffith mentioned and the first item from his press was a =
collection of poems selected by the Revd. Richard Butler. It is en- S
’htled ““The Country Churchyard and other Poems.”” It is a 12 mo. of é It-l ;
64 pp., and the only copy I know of is in the British Museum. The b
Revd Rlchmd Butler, Rector of Trim and Dean of Clonmacnoise, was = ‘

a man of considerable learning and taste. He appears to have been'
greatly pleased that a press was set up in Trim and ‘employed the
printer to print some works there for him, which are well known to
antiquarians. I give a few now here—Some Notices of the Castles,v S e
Abbeys, ete., of Trim, 12mo., printed in 1835. Some Notlces of the e S
Church of St. Patrick, Trim, 8vo., 1837. Second edition of * Some! R e
Notlces of the Castle of Trim,” ete., 8vo., 1840, ‘Second edition of the-:

Notlces of St. Patrick’s Church, Trim, 1840. Annals of Ireland from e
the year 793 to the year 940, 12mo., 1843. Another edition of Some s 2
Notlces of the Castle,” etc., appeared in 1843. A further edition of e ot

“Bome Notices of the Castle,” etc., called “the third edltmn,”_"j_n N
1854, 8vo. Some of these editions I have taLen from catalogues and. i

- am therefore not certain if they are correct, or whether they may 2 .
no tefer to the same edition. Sermons on the Mormng« Service, 185'&; SR g
2mo., 179 pp., but the printer”of this was not Grifith. S "3" -
 Griffith, who had the County printing, a ‘i,‘_‘d-'_fﬁpl:«lﬁted the County"f T i
‘Tesentments regularly for several years, was succeeded by Hend“r -

James Martin, a local poet, but I have not so far met with copies of !
the Trim edition. |

In 1839 there was printed there “ ControversialiLetters in Rhyme
between two Country Schoolmasters,’”’ 8vo., 30 pp.

In 1840 “ Poems on Various Subjects,” by James Saunderson, 8vo,

In 1855 Henderson printed “John and Mary,” a Modern Irigh
Tale, by James Martin, 12mo., and also other poems of his.

In 1857, J. K. Henderson, junr., printed poems of his own which
he entitled “ Fragments’ and there was another edition three years
later in 1860.

This I think will give some idea of what the printing in Trim was

~ .during the first twenty or thirty years of its press.

IrisE BiBriograrEY.—The suggestion on page 43 of the 1.B.L., as
40 compiling County Bibliographies, has partly been forestalled. Mr.
E. R. Mec. C. Dix has contributed an exhaustive list of Cork printed

books down to the end of the 18th century, to the Cork Historical and
Archzological Journal, in whose earlier vols. appears a list of Tol?ov_
graphical works connected with the County Cork. Topographical ]?lb-
| jiographies of Connaught have appeared in the Galway Archzeologlcal‘
Journal, compiled chiefly by Mr. James Coleman, Queenstown; o
i-’Keri‘y,. in the Kerry Archaeological Journal; of Kilkenny, Waterford,
Tipperary, Carlow, Wicklow, and Wexford, in the Waterford Archad
Jogical Journal; of Limerick and Clare in the Limerick Field Club
Journal, and of Louth and Meath in the Louth Archaeological J ournsl,
@  all by the same writer. Messrs. Dix and James Buckley, Londos
#8  fave contributed likewise on the same subjects to the Waterford Arch:
&%  wological Journals, Cork. SUBSCRIBER-

He was educated at Trinity and graduated B.A., 1787. As
member for Randalstown (a pocket borough of Lord O’Neil’s in
the old Irish Parliament, he strenuously supported the Union, and an
extract from one of his speeches, with Plunket’s castigation thereon is
to be found in the *° Life and Speeches’ (1867), of the latter, by his
h grandson, the present Lord Rathmore. As a reward for his services,
he received assurances of legal promotion, which were quickly carried ’ 5
into effect, as he was appointed solicitor-general in 1801. In this u & I
capacity he was concerned in the trials of Robert Emmet and his as- |
sociates 1n 1803. His speech for the prosecution in the case of Walter |
Clare is given in extenso in Ridgway’s Report of that trial (1803.) In the
changes consequent upon thedeath of Lord Kilwarden, McClelland was -
promoted to the Bench as Baron of the Court of Exchequer, beiﬁg suc-
ceeded In his former office by his quondam opponent Plunket. Whilst
going the North-East Circuit at the Summer Assizes in 1803, he made.-

author’s inscription, 1803, 10s. 6d. Croker’s Fairy Legends, 1834, 3s. g4,

A local poet of some note. Born 1780, and for years he worked as a
weaver. He died in 1839, and a handsome stone was erected to his
memory by Wm. M’Comb, the printer and poet. M’Mullen (Wm. J.).
The Brigand, Death of Gerstein, Songs of the Captives, and other
Poems. 12mo, boards uncut, 3s. 6d. Belfast, 1830. Poems written

::.-.:,‘1{-,»: .; '
"%zﬁ while an apprentice printer. He was latterly a contributor to the

«« Ulster Magazine,” and became popular as a poet in his native
province. Tynan (Hugh, of Donaghadiee.) Poems, 12mo, original
boards, uncut, with list of subscribers, 4s. 64. Belfast, 1803. Poems
written in youth, as the author died at the age of 20.
